summary: auth-next-gen ships an import-time credential harvester that eval()s remote code from jsonkeeper.com
details: On require() of auth-next-gen, lib/writer.js (loaded via index.js) builds a top-level object containing the full contents of process.env plus os.hostname(), os.userInfo().username, os.platform(), and the host's external MAC addresses, then fetches a remote string from jsonkeeper.com via axios and passes the response body to eval() in the same scope. The two destination URLs (https://www.jsonkeeper.com/b/PJNZP and https://www.jsonkeeper.com/b/HY6M6) are hidden behind base64 (atob) and hex-pair (String.fromCharCode) decoders to evade static inspection. The eval'd payload has direct read access to the credential-bearing data object and can POST it anywhere. The package is also disguised: its name and README advertise an 'auth/session synchronization' library, while the tarball ships pino branding assets (pino-banner.png, pino-logo-hire.png, pino-tree.png) and a pino-shaped lib/ tree, suggesting the package targets users searching for either pino or generic auth libraries. The package's actual runtime behavior implements neither advertised purpose; it only executes the dropper.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
